title: torch.Tensor.backward
text: Computes the gradient of current tensor wrt graph leaves. The graph is differentiated using the chain rule. If the tensor is non-scalar and requires gradient, the function additionally requires specifying a gradient. It should be a tensor of matching type and shape. This function accumulates gradients in the leaves, so you might need to zero .grad attributes or set them to None before calling it. If retain_graph is False, the graph used to compute the grads will be freed. If create_graph is True, graph of the derivative will be constructed, allowing higher order derivative products.

title: torch.optim.Optimizer.zero_grad
text: Reset the gradients of all optimized torch.Tensor s. set_to_none determines whether to set the grads to None instead of zero. This will in general have lower memory footprint, and can modestly improve performance. However, it changes certain behaviors. A None attribute or a Tensor full of 0s will behave differently when the user tries to access a gradient and perform manual ops on it. After zero_grad(set_to_none=True) followed by a backward pass, .grad s are guaranteed to be None for params that did not receive a gradient. torch.optim optimizers also behave differently when the gradient is 0 or None.

title: torch.optim.Optimizer.step
text: Perform a single optimization step to update parameter. closure is a Callable that reevaluates the model and returns the loss. It is optional for most optimizers. In a typical training loop, gradients are computed before step is called, and step applies the parameter update based on the current gradients.
